sections.heading: Vendor AI Due Diligence Checklist
paragraphs: A practical checklist for screening, validating, and documenting third-party AI vendors before purchase, renewal, or expanded deployment.
tables:
Purpose
Use this checklist to confirm that an AI vendor has been properly profiled, that data handling and security controls are understood, that AI-specific risks have been addressed, and that the organization can defend its buying decision if leadership, customers, auditors, or common sense ask hard questions later.
Before purchase, pilot, renewal, or expansion
Procurement, IT, security, privacy, legal, business sponsor

bullets: Use it as a process checklist, not as a substitute for judgment. A completed checklist with no thinking behind it is just paperwork cardio.
Capture evidence as you go: links, questionnaires, contracts, security reports, architecture notes, and remediation commitments.
Any vendor touching sensitive data, core business processes, or external-facing decisions should trigger deeper review before approval.
Open gaps do not always mean “do not buy,” but they do mean “do not pretend.” Track them and assign conditions, owners, and due dates.
sections.heading: How to use this checklist
Checklist flow and decision guidance
paragraphs: The checklist is organized in the order a sensible team would usually review a vendor: establish context, understand data and security posture, review AI-specific governance, then land the decision cleanly.
tables: Business need, sponsor, deployment path, contract timing
Data types, access model, storage, security controls, legal terms
Model governance, training practices, human oversight, drift and change control
Residual risks, remediation conditions, approval path, next actions

sections.heading: Suggested dispositions
tables: Low residual risk and controls are acceptable
Proceed with normal onboarding and register the vendor in the relevant inventory.
Document conditions, due dates, owners, and any usage restrictions before rollout.
Material risk, unclear evidence, or high-impact use case
Escalate to security, privacy, legal, executive sponsor, or AI governance forum.
Unacceptable risk or unresolved control weakness
Do not buy, renew, or expand until the issue is materially addressed.

Minimum evidence to request
At a minimum, request the vendor questionnaire or equivalent written responses, security and privacy documentation, architecture or data flow summary, contract terms, subprocessors list, incident response commitments, and any AI-specific governance material the vendor can produce without fainting.

sections.heading: 1. Engagement context and vendor profile
paragraphs: Confirm the organization knows what is being bought, who wants it, why it matters, and how the vendor fits into the environment before the due diligence gets lost in technical weeds.
tables: A business sponsor is identified and accountable
Business sponsor, team, and intended users are named.
The service or product being reviewed is clearly defined
Vendor name, product name, edition, modules, and deployment model are captured.
The intended use case is described in plain language
Business objective, user population, and whether the tool is internal or external facing are recorded.
New purchase, pilot, renewal, expansion, replacement, or emergency exception is identified.
Key dates and commercial timing are understood
Desired go-live, contract timing, renewal date, and any urgent decision points are captured.
Planned integrations, SSO, API access, data connectors, or embedded model usage are listed.
The vendor has a named point of contact for diligence
Sales, security, privacy, or technical contacts are available for follow-up.

sections.heading: 2. Data handling, privacy, and information lifecycle
paragraphs: This section checks whether the vendor touches the wrong data in the wrong way, which is usually how a “cool tool” becomes a compliance migraine.
tables: Data categories processed by the vendor are identified
Public, internal, confidential, regulated, customer, employee, source code, or other data types are listed.
Sensitive or regulated data exposure is explicitly assessed
PII, PHI, payment data, legal records, or other sensitive inputs are identified or ruled out.
The vendor’s data flow is understood
Where data enters, is processed, stored, transferred, and deleted is documented.
Training on customer data is confirmed or contractually restricted
Clarify whether prompts, files, telemetry, or outputs are used to train or improve the vendor’s models.
Retention and deletion terms are reviewed
Retention windows, deletion rights, backup handling, and offboarding deletion process are captured.
Subprocessors and hosting locations are disclosed
Material subprocessors, storage regions, and cross-border data transfer considerations are documented.
Privacy commitments align with intended use
DPA, privacy notice, lawful processing basis, and data subject rights support are reviewed where applicable.

sections.heading: 3. Security, identity, access, and resilience
paragraphs: This section covers the controls that keep the vendor from becoming an open side door into your environment or a black box that collapses the first time something goes wrong.
tables: Identity and authentication model is reviewed
SSO support, MFA, local account controls, service accounts, and provisioning methods are documented.
Role-based access and admin control model are understood
Privilege levels, admin roles, separation of duties, and access restriction capabilities are captured.
Security assurance evidence is requested and reviewed
SOC 2, ISO 27001, penetration tests, certifications, or security whitepapers are gathered.
Encryption in transit and at rest, key management approach, and tenant segregation position are reviewed.
Logging and audit visibility are available or planned
Admin logs, user activity logs, API logs, and export options are confirmed.
Incident response and breach commitments are documented
Notification terms, response obligations, and support paths for incidents are captured.
Business continuity and service resilience are assessed
Availability commitments, backups, failover, disaster recovery, and support model are reviewed.

sections.heading: 4. AI-specific governance and model risk controls
paragraphs: A normal SaaS review is not enough when the vendor’s product uses AI. This section tests whether the vendor can explain how the magic trick works without acting offended that you asked.
tables: The AI capability used in the product is clearly described
Foundation model, embedded feature, agentic workflow, retrieval system, or custom model usage is identified.
Confirm whether outputs require review before action and whether the tool supports reviewer checkpoints.
High-impact decision use is identified or prohibited
Clarify whether outputs influence hiring, legal, financial, safety, customer, or regulated decisions.
Testing, validation, or quality control practices are described
Vendor-provided evaluation methods, benchmark claims, or quality monitoring processes are captured.
How the vendor handles model upgrades, feature changes, release notes, and customer notice is reviewed.
Bias, safety, and misuse safeguards are addressed
Content filtering, guardrails, abuse prevention, safety mitigations, and known limitations are documented.
Explainability, transparency, or documentation is sufficient for the use case
Assess whether the vendor provides enough information for the organization to trust and govern the output.

sections.heading: 5. Legal, contractual, and commercial review
paragraphs: This section confirms the contract does not quietly undo the control posture you thought you had five pages ago.
tables: Contract terms align with the intended risk posture
Master terms, order form, and any AI-specific addenda are reviewed.
Data ownership and usage rights are clear
The organization retains ownership of data, outputs, and restricted use rights are documented.
Contract language protects prompts, uploaded files, outputs, and related business information.
Audit, assurance, or evidence rights are understood
Determine whether the organization can obtain updated reports, questionnaires, or notice of material changes.
Liability, indemnity, and limitations are reviewed
Commercial and legal stakeholders assess whether the balance is acceptable for the use case.
Termination and exit terms are workable
Data export, deletion support, notice periods, and dependency risks are reviewed.
Pricing and commercial model do not introduce hidden risk
Metering, usage caps, overage behavior, and expansion assumptions are understood.

sections.heading: 6. Implementation readiness, conditions, and handoff
paragraphs: Before anything is approved, make sure the organization is actually ready to deploy the vendor without inventing controls later under stress.
tables: Identify who will lead onboarding, configuration, and control implementation.
Required technical controls are known before purchase
SSO, tenant configuration, logging, role restrictions, DLP, or network restrictions are identified.
User guidance or acceptable use needs are identified
Determine whether employees need training, policy updates, or restricted use guidance.
Summarize open issues, caveats, and assumptions rather than burying them in side comments.
Conditions of use are defined if approval is conditional
List restricted data types, prohibited use cases, compensating controls, or time-boxed approvals.
Registry and inventory updates are planned
Confirm where the vendor, tool, and related use cases will be recorded after approval.
Renewal or re-review triggers are defined
Set what would trigger re-review: new integrations, new data types, major model change, incident, or expanded scope.

sections.heading: 7. Final review, conditions, and sign-off
paragraphs: Use this page to close the loop, document residual risk, and make the decision readable for the next person who inherits the file instead of guessing what happened.
tables: Approve / Approve w/ conditions / Escalate / Decline

sections.heading: Approval and conditions checklist
tables: Decision authority is clearly identified before procurement proceeds
Name the approver or forum that owns the final decision.
Any conditions of approval are documented in writing
List conditions, restrictions, and due dates rather than trusting future memory.
Contract, privacy, and security commitments are stored with the review record
Reference the contract file, security packet, or repository location.
Vendor onboarding or rollout steps are aligned to the final decision
Confirm whether rollout may proceed, is limited, or is paused.
A follow-up review trigger is set
Examples: renewal, scope change, new data types, customer-facing expansion, material incident.
